title: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
display_title: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
wikitext:
[]: Has Evangelion changed? Or is it the audience?             Quote Post by goldenvalley » Wed Sep 04, 2013 10:39 pm Or more specifically, has the way the audience consumes Evangelion changed? I'm a bit of an old man when it comes to Eva. I was involved in a lot of translation and information gathering on the old Evangelion Mailing List in the late 90s/early 2000s. Not that any of this matters. (As lot you guys have done a much better job of pulling apart the series as time went on.) But it might give some context to my post. That aside, I'm a bit surprised that Evangelion 3.0 is being received like it is by diehard Evangelion fans. 3.0 is classic Anno. It defies the audience's expectations, it challenges you to call it "lazy", "poorly written" and "difficult to understand". Sometimes it does all of these things on purpose. And when it's over, you're left with more questions than answers and a mix of emotions. Back in the 90s when the show was aired, the reactions were a lot of the same as I'm seeing for 3.0. Anger, claims of Anno being a hack, claims that "Evangelion is ruined" or just general negativity. I wouldn't be shocked if Anno was attempting to bottle that lightning again and I'd go as far as saying he succeeded. For me, it seems more that maybe we as Evangelion fans have become comfortable having most of the answers for the past decade or so. Retroactively, with Wikis, lightning fast translation groups, tightly knit communities and more resources than you'd ever need; Evangelion makes sense if you do a little reading. But, Evangelion, as it was coming out, was ALWAYS confusing, it always appeared to be on the border of being badly written. It was always doing the opposite of what you'd anticipate. It felt like abstract art. You wondered whether the creator's intentions were what you were seeing or whether it was just a last minute hack job by someone who doesn't know how to paint. Episodes 25-26 garnered a response back then not unlike what I'm seeing now; minus the death threats and absolute insanity. And today, most fans have come to love them. I guess that's why I'm confused. Because when I finished 3.0, I had the same "What the hell did I just watch?" feeling I did when I had first finished watching End of Evangelion on a weird VHS bootleg. Have anime fans become more critical of presentation than they were when the show was released? Sorry if this post is in the wrong spot. Please merge if it is!

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Sep 04, 2013 11:29 pm There's also the fact that the new Eva films are being consumed at a rate of one every 2 - 3 years, rather than an episode every week and a full movie one year later. The amount of time that audiences have to maintain interest, stamina, and cognitive activity has increased greatly.

[]:             Quote Post by Guy Nacks » Thu Sep 05, 2013 1:19 am It's not terribly hard to nail down why people are having issues with the new films. I'll list a few points: 1. Different Formats: The series was 26 episodes long at 22 minutes each plus a finale movie clocking in at about 90 minutes for a grand total of around 660 minutes or 11 hours of screentime. 1.0-3.0 clockled in at about 315 minutes. 4.0 I expect will be around 105 minutes long, for a grand total of 420 minutes. The extra screentime that the series had allowed more characterization for various supporting characters, which gave the show an added layer of depth that lacks minorly in 1.0 and 2.0 and severely in 3.0. When I watched evangelion for the first time in the fall of 2009, I was astounded how all of the characters seemed like real people, very rounded and solidly characterized with scenes that gave us tidbits that made them feel human and not just drawings on a cel. Scenes or sequences like: Shinji's encounter with Kensuke in episode 4 Ritsuko's scenes with SEELE and Misato in episode 23 Toji's visit to Rei's apartment in episode 17 Hikari's visit to Toji's hospital bedside in ep 19 Fuyutsuki's and Gendo's backstory throughout most of ep 21 Note that I didn't include scenes with Shinji, Misato, Asuka, or Rei because, as far as I'm concerned, they are the show's most central characters and character-fleshing scenes with them are obligatory. Also note that none of these aforementioned scenes appear anywhere in Rebuild. All supporting characters that we had grown to like and/or sympathize with for those scenes amongst others similar in the series aren't given that level of characterization in Rebuild, therefore we don't care as much about their characters and thusly the films lack the depth of characterization that was in the series. Could scenes like these have been in the rebuild films? Of course. But they deliberately chose not to include them. 2. Adaptation? Or new launching point? At this point I think everyone generally agrees that the objective of the Rebuild series has changed over the course of it's run. Originally, it was supposed to have been a straight up theatrical remake of the series with a larger budget and the promise of a new ending. However, at some point after 1.0 began production, Khara supposedly wanted to tinker with elements and alter them from what we'd originally been given in the series in a drastic manner. This is exemplified by the shot of Mari in the 2.0 teaser. 2.0 then, while not the near carbon copy of NGE that 1.0 was, still stuck to the series pretty significantly, with the only real alterations being the finale of the Zeruel battle, Asuka in unit 03, the trip to the water filtration plant thingy with the supporting cast, and the introduction of Mari. We're pretty much still getting what we got almost blow by blow in the original series as far as plot goes. 3.0 on the other hand throws the script for NGE almost entirely out the window with Kaworu's demise being the only real carryover. Characters that had been set up for 2 movies were now completely altered or unceremoniously dumped. The tone is completely different. The world is completely different. It's honestly at this point that I ask myself: If Anno really wanted to "change eva", then why didn't he make such drastic changes with the very first film? Why were we baited and switched with the trailer for Q and what 3.0 actually delivered? Was it deliberate by Anno? There are reports of 3.0's original story being scrapped midway through production which would account for the extended gap between Ha and Q, but have these stories been confirmed without a shadow of a doubt? In a film series, each film is supposed to feel important in some way, however now 1.0 feels like it almost doesn't even matter because it doesn't feel thematically connected with 2.0 and especially 3.0 given how the morals of rebuild differ with the morals of NGE (see that thread for discussion). 3. The Near Third Impact This point primarily regards 3.0. At the end of 2.0 Shinji initiates Third Impact.....only to be stopped about 60 seconds later by Kaworu's lance. There's a quick cut to Misato and co. who all look exactly the same as they did before Third Impact started.....no injuries or anything. Gendo, Fuyutsuki, Kaji, and Mari are all scattered nearby. There is no real indication that Near Third Impact caused as much damage as is explained by Kaworu in Q. Shinji has an excuse to not question him: He was in fantasyland with Rei in unit 01. But we the viewers saw exactly what happened and scratch our heads in confusion because it feels like Anno is retconning the events of the previous film (ironically much the same way another film with the phrase "Quickening" in it's title did some 20 years previous.....and we all know how well THAT turned out). If he isn't retconning, then there's something massively important to the plot that we're not being told. I'm not saying that we need EVERYTHING explained to us, but something as significant to the plot as the amount of destruction being shown in 2.0 vs. 3.0 really required answering, or else it doesn't make sense and we as an audience call bullshit. 4. Idiot Ball in Q This has been delved into many times by many on this forum including myself. Mainly regarding Shinji's nonsensical ignorance of Kaworu's pleading before pulling the spears.

[]: Re: Has Evangelion changed? Or is it the audience?             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Sep 05, 2013 1:39 am goldenvalley wrote: That aside, I'm a bit surprised that Evangelion 3.0 is being received like it is by diehard Evangelion fans.

[]:             Quote Post by Warren Peace » Thu Sep 05, 2013 5:07 am Well said, goldenvalley. What interests me is the way Rebuild has become, for some, a way of airing seemingly long-standing grievances concerning Evangelion as a whole. "Rebuild's merch is shamelessly whoring out the franchise!" we say, even after fifteen years of maid's outfit figurines and key chains. "Q is an affront to storytelling 101!" we say, even after episodes 25 & 26. "All this fan service is shameful!" we say, even after... well, the Misato shots in those first two episodes alone. I'm not saying all that isn't worth talking about, or potentially off-putting but, it's like... you're taking up torches and pitchforks now ? It makes me think there's more to it than just Rebuild. That's borne out through the increased personal attacks on Anno. Everyone wants to be the guy's psychiatrist, apparently. Or his inquisitor. There's a love/hate dynamic flowing through much of fandom, and Rebuild has become an excuse to let certain overflowing floodgates burst forth.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu Sep 05, 2013 6:02 am Did the audience really changed? From what I understand from the veterans here, the reactions were as visceral, if no more, when EoTV was aired. IMHO, what happened is that everyone now know about the surprise of EoTV, and thought that since 1.0 and 2.0 are "just" retelling of NGE with shiny 3D CGI and some changes left and right(Red Sea, Mari, Kaworu in the Moon...), and that at this point everyone thought that Anno couldn't pull off a surprise as big as EoTV, in the end we will have a similar story that the end of NGE, with different ending(not really that difficult, the manga did the same... with the result we know) ... and then turns out that Anno still can still completely surprise the audience, except that instead of a big mindfuck made of of people sitting in chairs, rough drawings and alternative universe, he pulled it off in a less mindscrewy but as effective way with a surprise time skip and change of setting, giving us would be described as a spiritual sequel of EoE in the ravaged post-3I world, except that in Rebuild things are even worse! If I refer to Guy Nacks's post, most of the complains comes from the fact we don't understand Q, the events that happened during the time skip, the changes in almost every characters, the narrative change, extremely centered on Shinji... but are all of these complains a little premature? After all there is still a movie left, and 3.0 obviously serves as a prologue for FINAL, so of course everything is not explained, we are confused, so we will want to know the rest in the final movie! Isn't that situation very similar than when EoTV first aired and everyone was waiting for the movie that will finally explain to us WTF is going on and what the hell did we see previously? So in the end, I don't think that the audience changed that much, it's just that now everyone has Internet, and with this HD, screenshots, forums and whatnot, the fans' voices are far easier to hear, it's as simple as that!

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Thu Sep 05, 2013 8:10 am My general feeling is that Ha was the real deal-breaker, not Q. 2.22 is where everything went off the tracks, suddenly making a bizarre mix of directorial decisions that give us what appear to be entirely useless scenes like the water park, the bafflingly stupid cooking subplot, utterly pointless fanservice shots, and brand new characters who don't seem to have a vaid reason for existing in the first place. In exchange we gain... well, almost all of the characterization is gone, we have to suffer through some moments of Anno trying his hand at fanfiction plotting, and Shinji going super saiyan. At least the Man Who Stole the Sun montage was nice, and the battles were mostly neat? Sigh. Q leaves far more questions than answers and generally spends most of it's effort pissing me off , but it does it in a way that has reengaged me with the story. That said, I expect to be unsatisfied by final; I just don't believe it's ever going to answer the many, many questions the previous films have raised and I have a sneaking suspicion Shinji is just going to disappoint me again like he did in EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Sep 05, 2013 9:13 am NemZ has probably hit the nail on the head as far as the problem with 2.0 goes. The way I see it, 2.0 was somewhere in an 'uncanny valley' - similar enough to the old series and yet different enough, with changes that were indeed dubious (the loss of characterization, gratuitous subplots and old-fashioned over-the-top silliness) that it really caused a fracture in the fanbase. 3.0 didn't even pretend to be anything like the original series. Throw in a massive timeskip during which a lot of plot-critical events take place, a gargantuan semi-organic flying warship, and new Evas and the audience is thrown into Shinji's position. Anno is effectively trolling the audience with Shinji's actions - even when Shinji tries to bring the original world back, his actions almost cause another impact and very nearly screw it up further. There's no way out but to move in a different direction. All the would-be haters are so stupefied by the change in direction that they really can't hate it any longer, because they're too busy trying to figure out what the hell is going on to hate it.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Thu Sep 05, 2013 9:35 am Anno created a world filled to the brim with strange symbols, hidden meanings and allegories that no one seem to get, or find a reason for. He created purposefully flawed people (characters), to put them in a situation, that will cause them to make mistakes, so he can disproportionally punish them for the mistakes they made by his design, for reasons only he knows. And apparently it's all so he can give a message (in this case to us), that no one seems to truly understand, because it's purposefully made cryptic and make us feel it's beyond our "mortal comprehention". It's exactly like Bible and Anno is emulating God, with the way he treats his characters and how he is voicing his "logic" to us. And it's seem even more prominent in Rebuild than in original series. That also can be a reason why so many called it pretentious. It's not the everpresent crosses. It's that Anno tries to mimic God, just so he can get attention and made us listen to his current displeasures. I guess that's what irritates me at least.

[]: Re: Has Evangelion changed? Or is it the audience?             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Sep 05, 2013 9:40 am goldenvalley wrote: That aside, I'm a bit surprised that Evangelion 3.0 is being received like it is by diehard Evangelion fans.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu Sep 05, 2013 11:05 am Well if(emphasize on the "if") the whole time skip and apocalypse thing was planned since the beginning(or at least a similar big mood switch), it's possible that 2.0 being all fanfic-y was the whole point, to lure th audience in a false sense of security and put a big contrast with the next movie : basically the first half of 2.0 could be resumed as "cute teenagers doing cute things" : visit of the aquarium, the Rei/Shinji bonding in said aquarium, the Asuka/Shinji bonding in bed(... taken out of context this phrase can be very funny!), the kids at schools, the (one sided)cooking war, "poka poka" Rei... Even when in the main plot it goes all crazy : we have Mari, the new pilot completely crazy awesome who pilot just because she fucking enjoy it, completely new Angels that serves as punching-ball for the new pilots(Tunniel for Mari, Clockiel for Asuka), fan favorite Kaworu in a secret place waiting for the end of the construction of a "super-Evangelion"(Mark.06), Shinji who acts as a super-badass against Sahaquiel(which even makes Asuka have a crush on him as a bonus!) It's like most of the fan wanted to see for the characters of NGE after everything that happened to them : badass heroes, crazy awesome pilots, curb-stomb fights, romance... it's like ANIMA, but animated and without the watermelon boobs and lolis Rei and Mari! So the audience feels that they will get a more light-hearted version of Evangelion, where everyone don't have to pass to the mental meat-grinder and find their happiness... and then comes Bardiel, which remember us that we are still in Evangelion and not ISIK. But even then, once the incident is closed, the crazy awesome stuff is back for the next Angel, with Mari coming back with her craziness, the even more insane Beast Mode, and of course Shinji going all Chuck Norris and transforming his Eva into an unstoppable machine of death able to do never seen before things with the AT Field and throwing super-powerful eyebeams! And then Third Impact ! It's like Anno deliberately crammed as much nods to the more memorable scenes of EoE in the second half : EVA-03 with Asuka getting reduced to pieces, like EVA-02 in EoE(even going as far as having EVA-01 gut it with its teeth like the harpies did, and Asuka coming back with a damaged eye!), EVA-01 becoming a godlike being, but this time having the opportunity to show it's powers(Red EVA-01 vs Zeruel) and of course 3I. All of this for pure fan-service purpose! ... and then Q comes around and serves as an hard back at reality : there is no more time for doing cute things anymore, the only thing we see Asuka, Mari and Misato(and everyone else in WILLE) is fighting and being professional, no more time for slacking off and spinning in chairs, the situation is critical! The only cutes thing we see in this movie is Shinji bringing books to Rei Q(which only serves to highlight how something is wrong with her), Shinji playing the piano with Kaworu(which was actually a training of synchronization for EVA-13), and the both looking at the stars, both last things feeling completely out of place with them being at the bottom of what was EVA-01's cage in the middle of the ruins of NERV HQ! The fact that these three activities includes Shinji serving only to highlight how Shinji is still stuck in the past, where there was time to be as carefree, and how Kaworu is out of time of space. Someone in another thread posted the link of an interview of Anno where he lamented that since the second half of the 90's, the anime and manga industry ceased to tell it's audience a "Tale" to concentrate in the characters, putting the tale at the background(at best), and lamenting how his own work with Evangelion heavily contributed in that, with all the "Tale"(SEELE and Gendo's war, the good guys attempts to uncover the truth and thwart the conspiracy, Instrumentality) only being a pretext, a tool to showcase Shinji learning to love himself and embrace reality. And here in Q and the teaser for FINAL, we have the contrary : Q is very Shinji centric, but in the end even thought what his fate is sad and how he will come out of his despair will be important for him, what's important is that there is a world to save, and Gendo to stop, and unlike in EoE Shinji isn't the only one who can do something about this(even though he chose to do nothing until the very last moment, once inside Instrumentality); there is a whole organization working on that, and Shinji will eventually have the choice to move on from his despair and try to join them in the fight for mankind, or stay in a side crawled in fetal position, but WILLE won't wait for him, because there is more important things, like saving the world! Even the preview implicitly acknowledged it by dedicating only two lines to Shinji(he lost the will to live, he will find hope in his destination), the rest telling what will WILLE do to save mankind, because the "Tale" is what it's important, when we focus too much into the characters, especially only one, we miss the greater picture and that ends up deserving everyone, as Q showed us!

[]: Re: Has Evangelion changed? Or is it the audience?             Quote Post by Ornette » Thu Sep 05, 2013 11:25 am Bagheera wrote: These two quotes confuse me. If the reaction to Q is much the same as the reaction to EoTV/EoE in the 90s, how do you figure either Eva or the audience have changed?

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 05, 2013 2:49 pm EoTV sucks because Eva's strength has always been in its amazing and complex visuals and there's nothing amazing or complex about a clip show interspersed with people sitting on folding metal chairs. That it no doubt loses much in translation only makes it worse. Rebuild is all about the visual pizazz. Anno gave the whole Earth a vagina dentata.

[]:             Quote Post by goldenvalley » Thu Sep 05, 2013 6:09 pm Wow. Lots of response, didn't expect that! So many good posts. GuyNacks SPOILER: Show

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Thu Sep 05, 2013 6:33 pm goldenvalley wrote: You rarely get answers for everything and people do stupid things because of emotions when they're smart enough to know better. That's real life. So why do we expect differently from our fiction? Especially that which tries to mirror how real human beings act?

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 05, 2013 7:46 pm In a work of fiction, there's two kinds of confusion. The correct kind is where the audience is confused about events, but understands that there is a framework behind the scenes that they don't understand, and the information they do get is presented in a digestible way. For example, many of Kubrick's films, or Gene Wolfe's novels, are very dense and layered and it's easy to read through or view them and have no real sense of what's going on without contemplation and research. The same is true of Eva, to a degree. It throws shit at you without directly explaining it, but there's clearly a framework of internal meaningand metaphor that can be pulled apart and digested. The other kind is the bad kind, which is simply: The audience is confused because nothing makes any fucking sense. If you're seeing big, unexplained discrepancies, like the clear difference between the ending of 2.0 and the devastation we see in 3.0, it's a sign that the writing is not as tight as it could be. The original Eva survived the fiat of the author pushing the plot in a particular direction to make a hamfisted point on the strength of its visuals. 2.0 is actually a very tightly constructed film; the water park and cooking subplots are not simple frivolities but are vitally important; the water purification plant lunch scene, for example, is rife with Last Supper imagery and allusion and food is symbolic of companionship and comfort throughout the whole thing; hence the Dummy System biting, rather than simply crushing, Asuka, in an inversion of the Last Supper rite at the water plant, much as the practice of feeding virgins (note, further, how Asuka is trussed up like a sacrifice in a bad B-movie before she meets her fate) to Moloch is an inverse, but related mystical current to the Last Supper (this is my blood and my body- the god dies to feed mortals rather than the other way around, much as Rei was going to spread comfort to Gendo and Shinjithrough food) Eating and the comfort of sharing meals is as important as (and linked to) the hands-as-self imagery of the original series (hence Rei and Asuka's cuts to their hands, and the replacement of the hand with the mouth during the Bardiel scene and the odd posing of the shut down Eva afterwards, which strongly suggests a pregnant belly; the monster has been quickened by the consumption of the virgin sacrifice) This is a point I must now meditate on, as I only just now uncovered it. The Evangelions of Rebuild are pagan monster gods that feed on suffering, in contrast to the Evangelions of NGE which are basically gigantic Golems of Prague.

[]:             Quote Post by goldenvalley » Thu Sep 05, 2013 8:13 pm If you're seeing big, unexplained discrepancies, like the clear difference between the ending of 2.0 and the devastation we see in 3.0, it's a sign that the writing is not as tight as it could be. The original Eva survived the fiat of the author pushing the plot in a particular direction to make a hamfisted point on the strength of its visuals.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Sep 05, 2013 8:25 pm goldenvalley wrote: I guess the question I keep coming back to is: Why do we need explanations for everything in a story like this?

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 05, 2013 8:26 pm We are supposed to be in the same shoes as Shinji, but confusing us with a blatant retcon to make us feel his confusion is a cheap trick. The first-drafty, made-up-as-they-went-along feel present in both NGE and Rebuild is the eternal bane of all serialized media, and is to an extent unavoidable. Bagheera is correct. I'm not misunderstanding or misinterpreting anything. I know exactly what the film is going for, I just see that it is lacking in its execution. I think they overshot the mark- a 14 year time skip would be confusing enough on its own. They could have dialed back the damage from N3I, and it would still be entirely plausible for WILLE to blame Shinji for what happened during the time skip. Q tosses aside narrative causality for a particular mood, and despite my post modernist leanings I'm not a fan of that; as I said, I regard it as a cheap trick that's lacking in nuance.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Sep 05, 2013 8:33 pm goldenvalley wrote: Imagine you started work at a new job. Do you immediately run up to your boss and ask him what the business was like 11 years ago? 13? 7? Imagine you saw an old friend. Are you going to request a play by play of all major events in the past 14 years, the motivations for those events and an explanation for everything?

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
[]: Has Evangelion changed? Or is it the audience?             Quote Post by goldenvalley » Wed Sep 04, 2013 10:39 pm Or more specifically, has the way the audience consumes Evangelion changed? I'm a bit of an old man when it comes to Eva. I was involved in a lot of translation and information gathering on the old Evangelion Mailing List in the late 90s/early 2000s. Not that any of this matters. (As lot you guys have done a much better job of pulling apart the series as time went on.) But it might give some context to my post. That aside, I'm a bit surprised that Evangelion 3.0 is being received like it is by diehard Evangelion fans. 3.0 is classic Anno. It defies the audience's expectations, it challenges you to call it "lazy", "poorly written" and "difficult to understand". Sometimes it does all of these things on purpose. And when it's over, you're left with more questions than answers and a mix of emotions. Back in the 90s when the show was aired, the reactions were a lot of the same as I'm seeing for 3.0. Anger, claims of Anno being a hack, claims that "Evangelion is ruined" or just general negativity. I wouldn't be shocked if Anno was attempting to bottle that lightning again and I'd go as far as saying he succeeded. For me, it seems more that maybe we as Evangelion fans have become comfortable having most of the answers for the past decade or so. Retroactively, with Wikis, lightning fast translation groups, tightly knit communities and more resources than you'd ever need; Evangelion makes sense if you do a little reading. But, Evangelion, as it was coming out, was ALWAYS confusing, it always appeared to be on the border of being badly written. It was always doing the opposite of what you'd anticipate. It felt like abstract art. You wondered whether the creator's intentions were what you were seeing or whether it was just a last minute hack job by someone who doesn't know how to paint. Episodes 25-26 garnered a response back then not unlike what I'm seeing now; minus the death threats and absolute insanity. And today, most fans have come to love them. I guess that's why I'm confused. Because when I finished 3.0, I had the

 back then not unlike what I'm seeing now; minus the death threats and absolute insanity. And today, most fans have come to love them. I guess that's why I'm confused. Because when I finished 3.0, I had the same "What the hell did I just watch?" feeling I did when I had first finished watching End of Evangelion on a weird VHS bootleg. Have anime fans become more critical of presentation than they were when the show was released? Sorry if this post is in the wrong spot. Please merge if it is!

The amount of time that audiences have to maintain interest, stamina, and cognitive activity has increased greatly.

At this point I think everyone generally agrees that the objective of the Rebuild series has changed over the course of it's run. Originally, it was supposed to have been a straight up theatrical remake of the series with a larger budget and the promise of a new ending. However, at some point after 1.0 began production, Khara supposedly wanted to tinker with elements and alter them from what we'd originally been given in the series in a drastic manner. This is exemplified by the shot of Mari in the 2.0 teaser. 2.0 then, while not the near carbon copy of NGE that 1.0 was, still stuck to the series pretty significantly, with the only real alterations being the finale of the Zeruel battle, Asuka in unit 03, the trip to the water filtration plant thingy with the supporting cast, and the introduction of Mari. We're pretty much still getting what we got almost blow by blow in the original series as far as plot goes. 3.0 on the other hand throws the script for NGE almost entirely out the window with Kaworu's demise being the only real carryover. Characters that had been set up for 2 movies were now completely altered or unceremoniously dumped. The tone is completely different. The world is completely different. It's honestly at this point that I ask myself: If Anno really wanted to "change eva", then why didn't he make

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 now completely altered or unceremoniously dumped. The tone is completely different. The world is completely different. It's honestly at this point that I ask myself: If Anno really wanted to "change eva", then why didn't he make such drastic changes with the very first film? Why were we baited and switched with the trailer for Q and what 3.0 actually delivered? Was it deliberate by Anno? There are reports of 3.0's original story being scrapped midway through production which would account for the extended gap between Ha and Q, but have these stories been confirmed without a shadow of a doubt? In a film series, each film is supposed to feel important in some way, however now 1.0 feels like it almost doesn't even matter because it doesn't feel thematically connected with 2.0 and especially 3.0 given how the morals of rebuild differ with the morals of NGE (see that thread for discussion). 3. The Near Third Impact This point primarily regards 3.0. At the end of 2.0 Shinji initiates Third Impact.....only to be stopped about 60 seconds later by Kaworu's lance. There's a quick cut to Misato and co. who all look exactly the same as they did before Third Impact started.....no injuries or anything. Gendo, Fuyutsuki, Kaji, and Mari are all scattered nearby. There is no real indication that Near Third Impact caused as much damage as is explained by Kaworu in Q. Shinji has an excuse to not question him: He was in fantasyland with Rei in unit 01. But we the viewers saw exactly what happened and scratch our heads in confusion because it feels like Anno is retconning the events of the previous film (ironically much the same way another film with the phrase "Quickening" in it's title did some 20 years previous.....and we all know how well THAT turned out). If he isn't retconning, then there's something massively important to the plot that we're not being told. I'm not saying that we need EVERYTHING explained to us, but something as significant to the plot as the amount of destruction being shown in 2.0 vs. 3.0 really required answering, or else it doesn't make sense and we as an audience call bullshit. 4. Idiot Ball in Q This has been delved into many times by many on this forum including myself. Mainly regarding Shinji

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
.0 really required answering, or else it doesn't make sense and we as an audience call bullshit. 4. Idiot Ball in Q This has been delved into many times by many on this forum including myself. Mainly regarding Shinji's nonsensical ignorance of Kaworu's pleading before pulling the spears.

What interests me is the way Rebuild has become, for some, a way of airing seemingly long-standing grievances concerning Evangelion as a whole. "Rebuild's merch is shamelessly whoring out the franchise!" we say, even after fifteen years of maid's outfit figurines and key chains. "Q is an affront to storytelling 101!" we say, even after episodes 25 & 26. "All this fan service is shameful!" we say, even after... well, the Misato shots in those first two episodes alone. I'm not saying all that isn't worth talking about, or potentially off-putting but, it's like... you're taking up torches and pitchforks now ? It makes me think there's more to it than just Rebuild. That's borne out through the increased personal attacks on Anno. Everyone wants to be the guy's psychiatrist, apparently. Or his inquisitor. There's a love/hate dynamic flowing through much of fandom, and Rebuild has become an excuse to let certain overflowing floodgates burst forth.

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 of EoTV, and thought that since 1.0 and 2.0 are "just" retelling of NGE with shiny 3D CGI and some changes left and right(Red Sea, Mari, Kaworu in the Moon...), and that at this point everyone thought that Anno couldn't pull off a surprise as big as EoTV, in the end we will have a similar story that the end of NGE, with different ending(not really that difficult, the manga did the same... with the result we know) ... and then turns out that Anno still can still completely surprise the audience, except that instead of a big mindfuck made of of people sitting in chairs, rough drawings and alternative universe, he pulled it off in a less mindscrewy but as effective way with a surprise time skip and change of setting, giving us would be described as a spiritual sequel of EoE in the ravaged post-3I world, except that in Rebuild things are even worse! Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 a bizarre mix of directorial decisions that give us what appear to be entirely useless scenes like the water park, the bafflingly stupid cooking subplot, utterly pointless fanservice shots, and brand new characters who don't seem to have a vaid reason for existing in the first place. That said, I expect to be unsatisfied by final; I just don't believe it's ever going to answer the many, many questions the previous films have raised and I have a sneaking suspicion Shinji is just going to disappoint me again like he did in EoE.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Sep 05, 2013 9:13 am NemZ has probably hit the nail on the head as far as the problem with 2.0 goes. The way I see it, 2.0 was somewhere in an 'uncanny valley' - similar enough to the old series and yet different enough, with changes that were indeed dubious (the loss of characterization, gratuitous subplots and old-fashioned over-the-top silliness) that it really caused a fracture in the fanbase. 3.0 didn't even pretend to be anything like the original series. Throw in a massive timeskip during which a lot of plot-critical events take place, a gargantuan semi-organic flying warship, and new Evas and the audience is thrown into Shinji's position. Anno is effectively trolling the audience with Shinji's actions - even when Shinji tries to bring the original world back, his actions almost cause another impact and very nearly screw it up further. There's no way out but to move in a different direction. All the would-be haters are so stupefied by the change in direction that they really can't hate it any longer, because they're too busy trying to figure out what the hell is going on to hate it.

It's exactly like Bible and Anno is emulating God, with the way he treats his characters and how he is voicing his "logic" to us. And it's seem even more prominent in Rebuild than in original series. That also can be a reason why so many called it pretentious. It's not the everpresent crosses. It's that Anno tries to mimic God, just so he can get attention and made us listen to his current displeasures. I guess that's what irritates me at least.

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 could be resumed as "cute teenagers doing cute things" : visit of the aquarium, the Rei/Shinji bonding in said aquarium, the Asuka/Shinji bonding in bed(... taken out of context this phrase can be very funny!), the kids at schools, the (one sided)cooking war, "poka poka" Rei... Even when in the main plot it goes all crazy : we have Mari, the new pilot completely crazy awesome who pilot just because she fucking enjoy it, completely new Angels that serves as punching-ball for the new pilots(Tunniel for Mari, Clockiel for Asuka), fan favorite Kaworu in a secret place waiting for the end of the construction of a "super-Evangelion"(Mark.06), Shinji who acts as a super-badass against Sahaquiel(which even makes Asuka have a crush on him as a bonus!) It's like most of the fan wanted to see for the characters of NGE after everything that happened to them : badass heroes, crazy awesome pilots, curb-stomb fights, romance... it's like ANIMA, but animated and without the watermelon boobs and lolis Rei and Mari! So the audience feels that they will get a more light-hearted version of Evangelion, where everyone don't have to pass to the mental meat-grinder and find their happiness... and then comes Bardiel, which remember us that we are still in Evangelion and not ISIK. But even then, once the incident is closed, the crazy awesome stuff is back for the next Angel, with Mari coming back with her craziness, the even more insane Beast Mode, and of course Shinji going all Chuck Norris and transforming his Eva into an unstoppable machine of death able to do never seen before things with the AT Field and throwing super-powerful eyebeams! And then Third Impact ! It's like Anno deliberately crammed as much nods to the more memorable scenes of EoE in the second half : EVA-03 with Asuka getting reduced to pieces, like EVA-02 in EoE(even going as far as having EVA-01 gut it with its teeth like the harpies did, and Asuka coming back with a damaged eye!), EVA-01 becoming a godlike being, but this time having the opportunity to show it's powers(Red EVA-01 vs Zeruel) and of course 3I. All of this for pure fan-service purpose! ... and then Q comes around and serves

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 godlike being, but this time having the opportunity to show it's powers(Red EVA-01 vs Zeruel) and of course 3I. All of this for pure fan-service purpose! ... and then Q comes around and serves as an hard back at reality : there is no more time for doing cute things anymore, the only thing we see Asuka, Mari and Misato(and everyone else in WILLE) is fighting and being professional, no more time for slacking off and spinning in chairs, the situation is critical! The only cutes thing we see in this movie is Shinji bringing books to Rei Q(which only serves to highlight how something is wrong with her), Shinji playing the piano with Kaworu(which was actually a training of synchronization for EVA-13), and the both looking at the stars, both last things feeling completely out of place with them being at the bottom of what was EVA-01's cage in the middle of the ruins of NERV HQ! The fact that these three activities includes Shinji serving only to highlight how Shinji is still stuck in the past, where there was time to be as carefree, and how Kaworu is out of time of space. Someone in another thread posted the link of an interview of Anno where he lamented that since the second half of the 90's, the anime and manga industry ceased to tell it's audience a "Tale" to concentrate in the characters, putting the tale at the background(at best), and lamenting how his own work with Evangelion heavily contributed in that, with all the "Tale"(SEELE and Gendo's war, the good guys attempts to uncover the truth and thwart the conspiracy, Instrumentality) only being a pretext, a tool to showcase Shinji learning to love himself and embrace reality. And here in Q and the teaser for FINAL, we have the contrary : Q is very Shinji centric, but in the end even thought what his fate is sad and how he will come out of his despair will be important for him, what's important is that there is a world to save, and Gendo to stop, and unlike in EoE Shinji isn't the only one who can do something about this(even though he chose to do nothing until the very last moment, once inside Instrumentality); there is a whole organization working on that, and Shinji will eventually have the choice to move on from his despair and try to join them in the fight for mankind, or stay

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 to do nothing until the very last moment, once inside Instrumentality); there is a whole organization working on that, and Shinji will eventually have the choice to move on from his despair and try to join them in the fight for mankind, or stay in a side crawled in fetal position, but WILLE won't wait for him, because there is more important things, like saving the world! Even the preview implicitly acknowledged it by dedicating only two lines to Shinji(he lost the will to live, he will find hope in his destination), the rest telling what will WILLE do to save mankind, because the "Tale" is what it's important, when we focus too much into the characters, especially only one, we miss the greater picture and that ends up deserving everyone, as Q showed us!

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu Sep 05, 2013 7:46 pm In a work of fiction, there's two kinds of confusion. The correct kind is where the audience is confused about events, but understands that there is a framework behind the scenes that they don't understand, and the information they do get is presented in a digestible way. For example, many of Kubrick's films, or Gene Wolfe's novels, are very dense and layered and it's easy to read through or view them and have no real sense of what's going on without contemplation and research. The same is true of Eva, to a degree. It throws shit at you without directly explaining it, but there's clearly a framework of internal meaningand metaphor that can be pulled apart and digested. The other kind is the bad kind, which is simply: The audience is confused because nothing makes any fucking sense. If you're seeing big, unexplained discrepancies, like the clear difference between the ending of 2.0 and the devastation we see in 3.0, it's a sign that the writing is not as tight as it could be. The original Eva survived the fiat of the author pushing the plot in a particular direction to make a hamfisted point on the strength of its visuals. 2.0 is actually a very tightly constructed film; the water park and cooking subplots are not simple frivolities but are vitally important; the water purification plant lunch scene, for example, is rife with Last Supper imagery and allusion and food is symbolic of companionship and comfort throughout the whole thing; hence the Dummy System biting, rather than simply crushing, Asuka, in an inversion of the Last Supper rite at the water plant, much as the practice of feeding virgins (note, further, how Asuka is trussed up like a sacrifice in a bad B-movie before she meets her fate) to Moloch is an inverse, but related mystical current to the Last Supper (this is my blood and my body- the god dies to feed mortals rather than the other way around, much as Rei was going to spread comfort to Gendo and Shinjithrough food) Eating and the comfort of sharing meals is as important as (and linked to) the hands-as-self imagery of the original series (hence Rei and Asuka's cuts to

Article: Has Evangelion changed Or is it the audience
 was going to spread comfort to Gendo and Shinjithrough food) Eating and the comfort of sharing meals is as important as (and linked to) the hands-as-self imagery of the original series (hence Rei and Asuka's cuts to their hands, and the replacement of the hand with the mouth during the Bardiel scene and the odd posing of the shut down Eva afterwards, which strongly suggests a pregnant belly; the monster has been quickened by the consumption of the virgin sacrifice) This is a point I must now meditate on, as I only just now uncovered it. The Evangelions of Rebuild are pagan monster gods that feed on suffering, in contrast to the Evangelions of NGE which are basically gigantic Golems of Prague.

Q tosses aside narrative causality for a particular mood, and despite my post modernist leanings I'm not a fan of that; as I said, I regard it as a cheap trick that's lacking in nuance.
